title: What Is Price-to-Earnings Ratio? Definition, Formula & Examples
author: Sigma Capital
metaDescription: What is Price-to-Earnings Ratio? Learn the definition, formula, and real-world examples of P/E Ratio. Our comprehensive guide explains how P/E Ratio works and why it matters for investors.
excerpt: The P/E ratio measures a company's current share price relative to its earnings per share (EPS). It is calculated by dividing the market price per share by the EPS. A P/E of 15 means investors are wil...

content.definition: The P/E ratio measures a company's current share price relative to its earnings per share (EPS). It is calculated by dividing the market price per share by the EPS. A P/E of 15 means investors are willing to pay $15 for every $1 of earnings. This ratio helps investors assess whether a stock is overvalued or undervalued compared to its peers and historical averages.
content.formula: P/E Ratio = Share Price / Earnings Per Share
content.example: If Company XYZ trades at $150/share and earned $10/share in the last 12 months, its P/E ratio is 15x ($150 / $10). The S&P 500 historical average P/E is approximately 15-17x.
content.types: Trailing P/E uses past 12 months of earnings (more reliable), while Forward P/E uses projected future earnings (more speculative but forward-looking). A high P/E may indicate growth expectations or overvaluation; a low P/E may suggest value or declining prospects.
content.whyItMatters: Understanding P/E Ratio is essential for making informed investment decisions. Whether you are evaluating individual stocks, planning your portfolio allocation, or comparing investment options, P/E Ratio provides a quantitative framework that removes guesswork and helps you assess value objectively. Professional investors, analysts, and financial advisors all rely on P/E Ratio as a core metric in their decision-making process.
